snippets.title: DBSCAN with scikit-learn (scaled features)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[0.0, 0.0], [0.1, 0.2], [0.2, 0.1], [5.0, 5.0], [5.1, 5.2], [9.0, 9.0]])

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("scale", StandardScaler()),
    ("dbscan", DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=3, metric="euclidean")),
])

labels = pipe.fit_predict(X)
print("labels:", labels)  # noise is -1
print("n_clusters:", len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0))
snippets.explanation: Always scale before distance-based clustering. labels == -1 marks noise; cluster count is inferred, not passed as k.

snippets.title: k-distance plot to suggest eps
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
min_samples = 5
k = min_samples - 1  # distance to k-th neighbor

nn = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=k)
nn.fit(X_scaled)
distances, _ = nn.kneighbors(X_scaled)
k_dist = np.sort(distances[:, -1])

# Plot k_dist vs point index; elbow suggests eps
# import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; plt.plot(k_dist); plt.show()
print("90th percentile k-distance:", np.percentile(k_dist, 90))
snippets.explanation: Sort distances to the (min_samples−1)-th neighbor; an elbow in the curve is a common starting point for eps — validate on held-out views or domain maps.

snippets.title: Inspect core vs noise counts
snippets.code:
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN

model = DBSCAN(eps=0.4, min_samples=4).fit(X_scaled)
labels = model.labels_

n_noise = (labels == -1).sum()
n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
print(f"clusters={n_clusters}, noise={n_noise}, core_samples={model.core_sample_indices_.size}")
snippets.explanation: core_sample_indices_ lists core points that seeded clusters — useful when debugging too much noise or a single giant cluster.

snippets.title: Compare cluster count to k-means baseline
snippets.code:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, DBSCAN

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10).fit(X_scaled)
db = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=3).fit(X_scaled)

print("k-means labels (all assigned):", kmeans.labels_)
print("DBSCAN labels (-1 = noise):", db.labels_)
snippets.explanation: k-means forces every point into one of k groups; DBSCAN may leave noise unassigned and discovers k implicitly from density.

snippets.title: Sweep min_samples with fixed eps
snippets.code:
for min_samples in [3, 5, 10, 20]:
    labels = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=min_samples).fit(X_scaled).labels_
    n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
    noise_pct = 100 * (labels == -1).mean()
    print(f"min_samples={min_samples}: clusters={n_clusters}, noise={noise_pct:.1f}%")
snippets.explanation: Higher min_samples demands denser cores — often increases noise percentage and reduces cluster count for the same eps.
